main_summary.summary: Dorothy "Dottie" Foreman Zellner, recorded December 8, 2015 in Baltimore by Emilye Crosby for the Civil Rights History Project, recounts her trajectory from a New York Jewish immigrant left-wing household into SNCC's Atlanta headquarters. Born January 14, 1938 to parents who escaped Lithuanian and Ukrainian Jewish antisemitism, she came of age in McCarthy-era New York; the Rosenberg execution and her family's Smith Act prosecution shaped her political formation. After reading about the February 1960 Greensboro sit-ins, she joined CORE's June 1960 Miami workshop, was arrested at a New Orleans Woolworth sit-in, then took a Southern Regional Council research position in Atlanta in June 1961 producing the 18-month SRC sit-in report. After SRC fired her following an FBI visit, James Forman hired her at SNCC in October 1961. She typed field affidavits sent to Robert F. Kennedy, edited the Student Voice with Julian Bond, was hospitalized after a police beating in the Danville campaign, worked Mississippi Freedom Summer's WATS line in 1964, ran SNCC's Boston Northeast Regional Office, and in May 1967 co-authored with Bob Zellner the rejected proposal to retain white staff voting rights, after which they joined the Southern Conference Educational Fund.
key_themes: Jewish left-wing political formation and McCarthyism
SNCC administrative infrastructure and James Forman's leadership
Interracial organizing and the white-staff question
Press strategy and the Student Voice
Volunteer vetting for Mississippi Freedom Summer
main_summary.historical_significance:
Zellner offers a rare interior view of SNCC's Atlanta nerve center from October 1961 through 1967, the period when James Forman built the hub-and-wheel administrative architecture (Friends of SNCC support groups, field-secretary affidavit pipeline to Robert F. Kennedy, motor pool, campus travelers) that sustained Mississippi and Southwest Georgia projects. Her account corrects the historiographic record on Forman, whom she describes as containing crises in Greenwood, Mississippi and Danville, Virginia rather than the volatile portrayals in some published accounts and in the film Selma.

The testimony is also a primary source on the 1963 SNCC civil liberties workshop convened by Anne Braden and Ella Baker, a previously unsung decision to refuse loyalty oaths and to take the First Amendment rather than the Fifth if subpoenaed by HUAC. Zellner's recollection of the May 1967 Peg Leg Bates staff meeting (where the proposal she drafted with Bob Zellner was rejected) and her insistence on telling press the truth (including accurate demonstration counts) document SNCC's internal craft of organizing and communication.

chapters.title: Born to Immigrants
chapters.summary: Interviewed by Emilye Crosby in Baltimore in December 2015, Dottie Zellner traces her birth in New York in 1938 to immigrant Jewish parents, a father from Lithuania by way of England and Toronto who became a dentist because Jews could not teach in Canada, and a mother from Kiev, both secular leftists running a mom-and-pop practice out of their apartment.

chapters.title: The War Was Another Member of the Family
chapters.summary: Zellner recalls World War II as an ever-present specter in the household, the air-raid drills and blackout curtains, her eccentric father rushing between patients to catch the war news on the radio, and a newsreel of a maimed child that so frightened her she crawled under the theater seat.

chapters.title: You Never Throw Away a Book
chapters.summary: She remembers finding a book of concentration-camp photographs in her parents' library and, protective of her younger siblings, throwing it in the garbage, only to be scolded not for her terror but for discarding a book; underneath it all simmered a real, unspoken question of whether the Jews would survive.

chapters.title: How Did They Know Who We Were
chapters.summary: Zellner describes her parents' left politics, born of an anti-Semitic Toronto they had fled, and how their fear for Jews extended outward to other marginalized people; she recalls asking her father at eight how the Nazis knew who the Jews were, and realizing that they would always have known who a Black person was.

chapters.title: A Few Black Friends
chapters.summary: She recounts a childhood faux pas asking aloud on a bus why two Black strangers were not speaking, her parents' few Black friends and patients, and her father's lifelong devotion to the veterans of the Spanish Civil War, whom he treated free, a practice he later extended to civil rights workers.

chapters.title: His Jaw Was Wired Shut
chapters.summary: Zellner tells how her father unwired SNCC worker Bill Hansen's broken jaw, only for Hansen, a Catholic, to find it was a Friday and he could not have his longed-for steak, and recalls the left-wing friends who filled the house, including the one who armed her with a New York Times article to correct a teacher who denied the United States was aiding France in Vietnam in 1954.

chapters.title: Arrested Under the Smith Act
chapters.summary: She describes the wartime camaraderie dissipating by 1949, the Communist Party family in their building whose father was arrested under the Smith Act in 1951, the FBI tramping up the stairs at dawn, and her own sense of growing up marginalized, in a group of very unpopular people, under extreme McCarthyism.

chapters.title: The Rosenbergs
chapters.summary: Zellner recalls her high school nearly shutting down over a class wish to invite the atomic scientist J. Robert Oppenheimer to speak, and the signal event of her teenage life, the execution of Julius and Ethel Rosenberg, when she stood at fifteen picketing all night before the White House beside a young Mary Travers, soon of Peter, Paul and Mary.

chapters.part: The Warsaw Ghetto and the Sit-Ins
chapters.title: Bring As Many As You Can
chapters.summary: A teenage expert on the fate of the Jews, Zellner describes the indelible impression left by the Warsaw ghetto uprising, where desperate people held off the German army for six weeks, and the obligation she has carried since: that when you are up against the fascists with no other recourse, you must bring as many of them down as you can.

chapters.part: The Warsaw Ghetto and the Sit-Ins
chapters.title: This Was Our Moment
chapters.summary: Zellner recalls joining the NAACP in high school, glimpsing her hero Paul Robeson crossing Second Avenue, and the thrill of Brown v. Board and the bus boycott; in simple psychological terms, she says, the civil rights movement replaced the Jewish revolt she had experienced vicariously, becoming her own generation's moment.

chapters.part: The Warsaw Ghetto and the Sit-Ins
chapters.title: At the Counter
chapters.summary: She remembers exactly where she sat, at a coffee-shop counter near the 42nd Street library, when she read the small newspaper item about the Greensboro sit-ins in February 1960, electrified not that the students could not be served but that they did not get up.

chapters.part: The CORE Workshop and First Arrest
chapters.title: Your Time Will Come
chapters.summary: A college graduate living at home with a short-term job at the left-wing National Guardian, unsettled while friends married, Zellner recalls being told at a wedding that her time would come, and finding, through a friend, a CORE workshop she could enroll in rather than simply appearing in the South unannounced as others did.

chapters.part: The CORE Workshop and First Arrest
chapters.title: The Miami Workshop
chapters.summary: Zellner describes the June 1960 CORE workshop in Miami, four months after the sit-ins began, among people who became known, Rudy Lombard, Hugh Murray, the Stephens sisters, and Angeline Butler, and her sense looking back that the heavily Northern CORE was seeking a new generation of organizers and a wide geographical spread.

chapters.part: The CORE Workshop and First Arrest
chapters.title: The Only White Woman
chapters.summary: Zellner recounts the sit-in and arrest, realizing as they sang that she was the only white woman in the group, and the segregated jail where, petrified but not menaced, she lay on an unmade upper bunk and was handed a thirty-pound mop; she uses the day in her talks to show young people how, from waking to sleeping, an entire life was formulated on the basis of color.

chapters.part: The CORE Workshop and First Arrest
chapters.title: You Slept on That
chapters.summary: She describes the white women in the cell making ruthless fun of her, being called out to meet the lawyer and bonded out while some, like the Stephens sisters, chose to stay in, and her later realization, as an organizer, that no one had thought through what a lone white woman in jail might mean or discussed beforehand whether to remain.

chapters.part: The CORE Workshop and First Arrest
chapters.title: How Easy It Was
chapters.summary: Zellner recalls the role-playing of the nonviolence training, made to play the screaming attacker and unnerved by how easily one could become a fascist, and admits she had no consciousness of herself as a woman, following an unorthodox path that nonetheless assumed she would marry and have children.

chapters.title: Casing Woolworth's
chapters.summary: Begging to go on to New Orleans, Zellner stayed with a Black woman near Claiborne Avenue and helped prepare the city's first sit-in, which she notes came from the CORE people rather than the local students; with her art-school training she made the signs, cased the Woolworth's on Canal Street, and was sent to do white-community outreach, finding a willing ally in Father Joseph Fichter of Loyola.

chapters.title: The Rabbi Threw Me Out
chapters.summary: Zellner tells of the rabbi, Julian Feibelman, who threw her out of his office, a story she has used as a Jewish activist for fifty-five years, and of coming to understand the unenviable position of Southern Jews she met traveling the region, like the storekeeper in a Hayneville-style town, never accepted, always trying desperately and vainly to be white.

chapters.title: Go to SNCC
chapters.summary: Zellner recalls Bernard Lafayette advising her to go to SNCC, the spectacular New Orleans sit-ins where her assignment was to drive, and the outsized role of cars in a movement that had almost none; she learned that a woman never drove if a man was present, a theme full of legendary drivers and life-or-death escapes that runs through the book Hands on the Freedom Plow.

chapters.title: Stokely and the Gravel
chapters.summary: She paints Stokely Carmichael as inseparable from a car, roaring into the second district of Mississippi with the gravel flying before smoothing down his clothes, and confesses she herself was a terrible driver who, sent to drive after the New Orleans sit-in, nearly killed everyone in a traffic circle while they screamed; the same day Hugh Murray's father stormed in and dragged his son from the counter.

chapters.title: The Letters in the Garbage
chapters.summary: Back home, set on SNCC, Zellner wrote letters that went unanswered into the garbage for want of anyone to open them, worked a dreadful proofreading job and then the welfare department, and recounts how a fired Black professor she had defended as a student editor, James A. Moss, turned up as research director at the Southern Regional Council just as she applied.

chapters.title: My Name Was Not on It
chapters.summary: Hired in three weeks at the Southern Regional Council, Zellner did the research for its report on the first eighteen months of the sit-in movement, which landed on the front page of the New York Times under no name of hers, as was the style of the day; living on the white side of Atlanta, she led a double life, crossing to the Black side where she could.

chapters.title: The White Side and the Black Side
chapters.summary: Zellner reflects on the guilty double life of living white while her Black colleagues had to come to her house after dark, on rooming with Jane Bond when a woman renting alone was rare, and on the agony of knowing SNCC was twenty minutes away on foot, full of the people in her own reports, yet unreachable to her for months.

chapters.title: Can You Type
chapters.summary: Zellner recounts finally walking into SNCC in October 1961, a month after James Forman arrived, and the momentous question he asked, whether she could type, which feminists later deconstructed but which thrilled her; knowing a white Northern woman's options in the South were narrow, she happily typed, then told him she could write too, and he put her to work with Julian Bond.

chapters.title: The Switchboard in His Head
chapters.summary: Zellner describes Forman's genius as a switchboard in his head that held the whole organization, instantly knowing where each person was needed, and recalls typing the field secretaries' affidavits and, in time, writing his letters from a one-line instruction; she also explains the deadly combination, in the white mind, of a white woman and a Black man.

chapters.title: White Women Were Anathema
chapters.summary: She lays out the century of lynchings charged to Black men accused of raping white women, the famous cases like Willie McGee and Mack Charles Parker that were in fact relationships ending in betrayal and death, and how white women were anathema in the rural South, so that Charles Sherrod in Southwest Georgia treated them as charges about to be detonated.

chapters.title: Misconstrued by History
chapters.summary: Zellner argues that historians who never knew Forman have misconstrued him, a highly intelligent, cultured French speaker who read Africa before anyone, short on small talk but the inventor of the friends-of support groups and the hub-and-wheel model that served the rural work, with Ella Baker keeping SNCC autonomous.

chapters.title: A Laser Beam
chapters.summary: She describes typing the field affidavits, fast and inaccurate, that Forman sent off to Robert Kennedy on the theory that the 1957 Civil Rights Act already gave grounds to jail the registrars, and the rare human contact Forman made, like a laser beam, his fine teeth, his sense of humor, and his famous wind-it-up gesture at people who talked too long.

chapters.title: Hands on the Hood
chapters.summary: Zellner recalls two narrow escapes with Forman: a night near Greenwood in 1963 when interracial carloads were stopped and made to put their hands on the hood, and Forman handled the police with total calm; and Danville, where as they were about to be hosed and beaten he saw disaster coming and moved to prevent it.

chapters.title: Seeing Stars
chapters.summary: She describes the Danville pamphlet Forman sent her to write, the fire hoses that had already knocked her down, and the night a policeman struck the hundred-six-pound, shoeless woman over the head as she knelt, the blow making her see actual flashing lights, captured in Danny Lyon's photograph of Forman taking her affidavit.

chapters.title: Get Up and Get the Hell Out
chapters.summary: Zellner explains the SNCC method of always going back to where the danger was, and the day forty people sat on the city-hall steps facing deputized drunks and fire hoses while Forman, sensing they would go stir crazy, ran a Black-history workshop about Harriet Tubman, then distracted the police chief and, behind his back, signaled them all to rise with dignity and leave.

chapters.title: He Had the Stature to Retreat
chapters.summary: Zellner credits Forman twice with saving her life, having the stature to know when to retreat rather than let people be killed, and the nerve to buy twenty cars at once; she bitterly rejects the portrayals of him as petulant or unstrung, including in the film Selma, insisting he was brusque but truthful and never spared himself.

chapters.title: Then He Crashed
chapters.summary: Zellner reveals that through all the years she knew him Forman showed no sign of the psychiatric history he carried, until accumulated stress brought a series of breakdowns; his wife, Dinky, got him to Puerto Rico where Betita Martinez worked on his book, but a final refusal of treatment left him a changed man, even a poisoning fear she once met by offering to taste his food first.

chapters.title: He Gave Himself to Us
chapters.summary: She honors Forman as the man at the center of the hub who invented the campus travelers and the friends-of groups to serve the rural work, ruthless for publicity for the cause but never himself, and mourns that he has not been treated as the great man he was; his sons James Jr., a Yale professor, and Chaka, an actor, carry on, and she recalls a late flash of his old self over a claim to be Native American.

chapters.title: With God on Our Side
chapters.summary: Zellner recalls Pete Seeger and Bob Dylan at the 1963 Greenwood festival in a big field, Dylan singing his With God on Our Side, seemingly incomprehensible to an audience whose religious faith had held them together for three hundred years, and the whole Mississippi summer as a living nightmare from the moment you passed the welcome sign.

chapters.title: The FBI Came Asking
chapters.summary: Zellner recounts how the FBI came to Leslie Dunbar, head of the Southern Regional Council, with a photo of her, and how Dunbar fired her months later as redundant, a story she still weighs both ways; already in talks with Forman, who had no money to pay her, she now had her chance and joined the SNCC staff she had longed for.

chapters.title: This Is the Man Who Fired Me
chapters.summary: Years later Zellner took immoderate pleasure in introducing her daughter to Dunbar as the man who fired her when the FBI came, and notes he later fired another good organizer who also went to SNCC; she suspects the photo was from a Cuban Missile Crisis demonstration organized by Howard Zinn, who told her the FBI had every one of the twenty-three names.

chapters.title: Two Opposite People
chapters.summary: Zellner describes meshing at once with Julian Bond despite being opposites, she rigidly punctual and hard-driven, he relaxed and a lover of gossip and funny stories; he became the confidant she trusted with personal matters he never repeated, one of the funniest people who ever lived, and she keeps every email he ever sent her.

chapters.title: He Took Up for Me
chapters.summary: She recalls wishing in 1963 that the two of them had written a book, the meaningful day the conflict-averse Julian defended her in a staff meeting against criticism for her punctuality, and the disbelief of learning, from an early-morning message Dorothy Burlage sent, that he had died.

chapters.title: Always Tell the Truth
chapters.summary: Zellner describes laying out dummies of the Student Voice and the principle she and Julian taught volunteers and have followed since: always tell reporters the truth, never inflate a crowd of forty-two into five hundred, because the reporter will not return; some press people they genuinely liked and trusted.

chapters.title: We Said Negro Deliberately
chapters.summary: She recalls the live phone interviews where the witty Julian gave the quotable line and she gave the earnest facts, and the deliberate practice, strange-looking now, of identifying Black people by race in press releases so the public would know that Black people were leading and being arrested at the front of the movement.

chapters.title: A Staff Member, Not Friends Of
chapters.summary: Married to Bob Zellner in August 1963 after stints in Danville and Greenwood, Zellner went to Boston and built the Northeast regional SNCC office, insisting always that she was a staff member taking direction from Atlanta, not a volunteer-run Friends of SNCC group, and interviewed prospective Freedom Summer volunteers.

chapters.title: A Year's Leave
chapters.summary: Zellner recounts the year's leave when Bob managed the campaign of Robert Cook, an early anti-Vietnam-War candidate, in New Haven, with one of them always at staff meetings, and how, pregnant in 1965 when custom stopped a woman working after her seventh month, she stepped down from the office that Bob then ran, their daughter born that July before they returned to Atlanta.

chapters.title: I Went Down to Marry the Movement
chapters.summary: Zellner recalls the December 1966 Peg Leg Bates meeting, which Bob attended and where he abstained, and Betita Martinez's account as the most coherent of many contradictory ones; the substantive discussions about SNCC becoming all Black were held without white people, and learning of them left her, who had gone down to marry the movement, completely beside herself.

chapters.title: We Would Not Surrender Our Vote
chapters.summary: Determined not to disappear without a trace, Zellner wrote a statement proposing that she and Bob remain full voting staff members while organizing in the white community, arguing you cannot organize white people in a vacuum; she eyed the tolerant artists' town of Fairhope, Alabama, but feared they would be killed, a fear Bob's mother shared when she begged him not to bring the danger home.

chapters.title: One Vote for Us
chapters.summary: Zellner reflects that the chances were good they would have been killed organizing in white Alabama, citing the interracial organizing Robin Kelley documented and traces Martha Prescod Norman Noonan found decades later; the statement endured as a record, but the proposal was rejected, with only one vote in their favor, after Bob presented it to the central committee.

chapters.title: He Could Not Hold Them Back
chapters.summary: She remembers confronting Forman in his deep psychic distress before his first breakdown and his refusal to deny he disagreed with the change; his comfort zone, she believes, was an interracial organization led by Black people, not one swollen with white volunteers, and though he could not stop the shift, his silence spoke, and she calls SNCC's disappearance within three years a horrible tragedy for the country.

chapters.title: There Was No Plan B
chapters.summary: Zellner names the deep disillusionment after Atlantic City, and a faith in lowercase democracy that left SNCC unprepared with no plan B, as central to its fracturing; when their proposal failed she and Bob went to Anne Braden's SCEF in New Orleans, declining the Bradens' road-and-home division of labor, and after ten years as professional organizers became regular people, he a union carpenter, she a practical nurse.

chapters.title: What If SNCC Were Called
chapters.summary: Zellner describes the unsung but vital spring 1963 civil-liberties workshop, born of the bond between Anne Braden and Ella Baker, both survivors of a still-living McCarthyism, to decide what SNCC would do if subpoenaed; she lays out the cruel choice activists faced between the Fifth Amendment, which the press equated with guilt, and the First, which sent Carl Braden and Frank Wilkinson to jail.

chapters.title: They Would Not Cooperate
chapters.summary: Recalling the 1954 Eastland hearings that smeared the Southern Conference Educational Fund, Zellner describes the agonized workshop debate over loyalty oaths and volunteers' politics, and the stunning decision that SNCC would not cooperate and would go to jail rather than name names; she is convinced an undercover agent carried that resolve to a government that, Julian later heard, ran thousands of Black informers.

chapters.title: Are You Ready to Die
chapters.summary: Zellner recounts the hard-headed work of interviewing prospective Freedom Summer volunteers in black-and-white pairs against a COFO questionnaire, screening out adventurers and divas and writing candid reports, and the woman who told her decades later that being asked whether she was ready to die was one of the worst and most honest things she ever went through.

chapters.title: We Let Abbie Hoffman Through
chapters.summary: She admits the one she slipped up on was Abbie Hoffman, then in a suit and tie, who later went public gnashing his teeth about his treatment as a white person, as did Susan Brownmiller; Zellner refused all interviews about the white exit, unwilling to join the chorus crying over the poor white people, asking whether, after Atlantic City, anyone should be surprised.

chapters.title: We're Going to Win
chapters.summary: Zellner points to Bob Moses on the Atlantic City convention floor certain they would win, with no plan B if they did not, and argues SNCC's true legacy was teaching that a Black person could direct a white one and that changing the context changes behavior; for a few brief years, she says, its people were the only Americans to achieve a non-racial community, bound by the knowledge that any of them might take a bullet, until a profoundly racist country did them in.

chapters.title: Take Care of Each Other
chapters.summary: Zellner sees in SNCC a still-living reservoir of creativity willing to support Black liberation, and says she now carries its lessons into her work speaking out as a Jewish person on Israel and Palestine, and into teaching the young to take care of each other and send people away on the rest Forman invented, support a movement today lacks against vicious, hateful opposition.

chapters.title: Brave as a Lion
chapters.summary: Zellner remembers the murmur when Ruby Doris Smith Robinson came into the office with a reputation for being brave as a lion, how Ruby respected her as a hard worker without much liking her at first, and how Ruby, in effect second to Forman, did the personnel and financial work, an emotional woman scarred, Zellner believes, by the trauma of breaking rocks at the prison farm.

chapters.title: Then We Became Friends
chapters.summary: When Ruby fell ill, Zellner, unafraid of the sick, visited her week after week at a New York hospital where the once-fierce woman grew gentle and the two became friends who laughed and traded stories; Ruby died of a virulent leukemia around twenty-six, and at the funeral that straightened the hair she had always worn natural, Zellner broke down sobbing, later naming her own daughter Katherine Ruby Zellner.

chapters.title: The Men Were Petrified of Her
chapters.summary: Zellner recalls Ruby demanding to know where the floaters were and the men being petrified of her, and her own terror going into Freedom Summer, vetting people for a place she dreaded and half-hoped an accident would spare her; in Mississippi she worked the WATS line in eight-hour shifts as a contact for parents and the press, and helped with the Belafonte and Poitier tour, leaving for a New Orleans that felt, by comparison, like Paris.

chapters.title: They Wanted SNCC Out of the Way
chapters.summary: Zellner closes on Forman's blunt, gameless manners, the day Kennedy was shot when he called her in a panic that the assassin might have been one of theirs, and the winter of 1964 to 1965 when she inadvertently learned that a coalition of more acceptable civil rights groups, through Allard Lowenstein and his aide Barney Frank, was planning to make sure the next summer would not be controlled by a SNCC they found too far out.

loc_video.caption: Dorothy Zellner oral history interview conducted by Emilye Crosby in Baltimore, Maryland, 2015 December 08
